articles.headline: Science Just Rewrote the Rules on How Long You'll Live
articles.body: You've probably heard that only about 20% of your lifespan is written in your genes. Turns out that number was way off. A new study published in the journal Science by researchers at the Weizmann Institute found that genetics actually accounts for about 50% of how long we live. That's double what scientists believed for decades. The old estimate was based on people born in the 1800s, when accidents and infections killed a lot of people young. Strip out those external causes of death, and your DNA matters a whole lot more. The good news: the other half is still yours to shape.
articles.takeaway: You can't outrun your genes, but you can stack the odds in your favor with the habits you build right now.

articles.headline: The Trendy 'Longevity Drug' That's Quietly Killing Your Workout Gains
articles.body: Rapamycin is the hot longevity drug thousands of people are taking off-label to live longer. The problem? A new clinical trial called RAPA-EX-01, published in the Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle, just found it may be working against your workouts. Older adults taking rapamycin weekly alongside an exercise program gained less functional strength than the group doing the same workouts with a placebo. Both groups improved, but the drug group averaged about two fewer chair-stand reps at 13 weeks. The drug blocks a pathway your muscles need to grow and get stronger. Great if you want to live longer. Not great if you also want to stay capable.
articles.takeaway: Before adding any longevity supplement or drug to your routine, ask how it interacts with your training. Exercise is still the best anti-aging tool we have.
articles.source: Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle

articles.headline: Bad Sleep Isn't Just Making You Tired. It Might Be Aging Your Brain.
articles.body: If you're brushing off your sleep struggles as just a busy-parent thing, this one's for you. A study published in the journal Neurology followed nearly 2,800 adults for six years and found that people with chronic insomnia were 40% more likely to develop memory problems or early dementia. People who slept less than usual due to insomnia showed cognitive scores equivalent to being four years older right from the start of the study. One in nine adults has chronic insomnia. Sleep isn't a luxury you'll catch up on someday. It's the most underrated performance tool you have.
articles.takeaway: If you're struggling to sleep at least three nights a week, treat it like the health priority it is, not just a lifestyle inconvenience.

articles.headline: Your Fish Oil Supplement Might Not Be as Brain-Safe as You Think
articles.body: Fish oil is practically a wellness staple. But a new study out of the Medical University of South Carolina, published in Cell Reports, is pumping the brakes on one of its key ingredients. Researchers found that EPA, one of the main omega-3s in most fish oil supplements, may actually interfere with the brain's ability to repair itself after injury. In their models, higher EPA levels in the brain were linked to weaker repair and worse memory after mild head trauma. The other main omega-3, DHA, showed none of those effects. This doesn't mean toss your fish oil. It means the source and type of omega-3 you take matters more than you thought.
articles.takeaway: Look for fish oil supplements higher in DHA than EPA, and if you play contact sports or have had head injuries, talk to your doctor before dosing up on EPA-heavy blends.
articles.source: Cell Reports (Medical University of South Carolina)
